narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 53.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is just over the threshold we use for approval. This prediction is based on key details from your profile, including your low ranking in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack performance. Your LSAT score of 28 also sits well under the average of around 37, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.2 is noticeably lower than the usual 3.2. Coming from a middle-income family, identifying as a female Asian student planning full-time enrollment, these factors together painted a picture of higher risk in the model's eyes. When the model weighed everything, your third-year decile rank stood out as the strongest influence pushing the prediction toward failure, more so than any other aspect. Right behind it was your LSAT score, which had the next biggest pull in that direction, followed closely by your first-year decile rank. Your family income level, racial background, undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status rounded out the influences, each nudging the odds slightly toward failure in descending order of impact. Compared to most applicants—who boast higher grade deciles, stronger test scores, and GPAs—these elements in your case deviated enough to tip the scales. It's worth noting that while no single factor dooms an application, the combination here mirrored patterns seen in students who struggled with the bar. The good news is you can take control moving forward: focus on bolstering your academic foundation through targeted test prep, perhaps by retaking the LSAT to build a stronger score profile for future applications. Intensive study habits and bar exam-specific courses could address the gaps hinted at by your past grades and scores. Seeking mentorship or tutoring in weak subjects might help replicate the trajectories of higher-performing peers. With dedication, you can shift these predictions in your favor for the next opportunity. Keep pushing—you have the potential to rewrite this story.
